source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: In the number 4,367, what is the value of the digit 3?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Gives 3 instead of the value 300.

cognitive_load.justification: Direct place-value recognition with a four-digit integer and one target digit.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: In the number 2,334, what is the value of the digit 2?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Reads the digit rather than the thousands value it represents.

cognitive_load.justification: Extends the same task to a larger number with thousands.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Using the digits 3, 8, 2 and 7 once each, what is the greatest 4-digit number you can make?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Does not place the largest available digit in the thousands column.

cognitive_load.justification: Uses place value strategically to build the greatest number from a digit set.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: A pupil says the smallest 5-digit number you can make with 9, 4, 2, 1 and 0 is 09421. What is the correct 5-digit number?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Places 0 first and treats it as a valid 5-digit number.

cognitive_load.justification: Targets the common leading-zero error in smallest-number construction.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Write 48 in words.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Writes four eight or misspells the tens word structure.

cognitive_load.justification: Low-load translation from two-digit numeral to words from the first independent practice set.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Write 293 in words.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Drops the hundreds or misorders the tens and ones words.

cognitive_load.justification: Adds a hundreds layer while keeping the task as direct numeral-to-word translation.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Write 1,602 in words.
question.answer: one thousand six hundred and two

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Writes sixteen hundred and two or drops the thousands structure.

cognitive_load.justification: Requires handling a zero in the tens place correctly when writing words.

accepted_answers: one thousand six hundred and two

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Write “nineteen million, eight hundred and seventy-six thousand, five hundred and forty-three” in figures.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Places digits in the wrong thousands or millions groups.

cognitive_load.justification: Reteach bridges commas/grouping and large-number word structure back into figures.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: True or false: 512 > 502 shows that 512 is greater than 502.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Reads the inequality symbol the wrong way round.

cognitive_load.justification: Binary check of inequality interpretation using the source example.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Place the correct symbol between 273 and 298.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Chooses > because the learner focuses on the left number first instead of comparing values.

cognitive_load.justification: The interaction is symbol entry, so it is kept as a short response rather than padded MCQ.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: What is the smallest integer that makes the statement 489 < ____ true?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Gives 489 instead of the next integer above the boundary.

cognitive_load.justification: Builds from simple comparison into satisfying an inequality with the nearest valid integer.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Rewrite this false statement so it is true: 85 < 83

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Keeps the same symbol direction or changes the numbers instead of the relationship sign.

cognitive_load.justification: Targets symbol reversal directly using a source false-statement pattern.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Which word means from largest to smallest?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Confuses ascending and descending order labels.

cognitive_load.justification: Very short vocabulary check before full ordering tasks.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Order these integers in descending order: 872, 802, 870, 780, 827

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Places 802 before 827 after looking only at the hundreds column.

cognitive_load.justification: Uses the exact source list and requires full ordered output rather than option spotting.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Order these integers from smallest to largest: 11, 20, 9, 15, 14, 3

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Produces descending order when the prompt asks for ascending order.

cognitive_load.justification: Switches direction to ascending so the learner must attend to the prompt, not a single routine.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: A pupil orders 321, 301, 312, 213, 212 as 321, 312, 301, 212, 213. Which two numbers have been put in the wrong order at the end?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Stops comparing too early and reverses the final pair.

cognitive_load.justification: Reteach focuses on checking the last comparison rather than redoing a full list from scratch.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: What does median mean?
options: The value in the middle of an ordered list
The largest value in a list
The total of all the values
The difference between the largest and smallest values
question.answer: The value in the middle of an ordered list

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Confuses median with range or total.

cognitive_load.justification: Checks the central definition before multi-step median tasks.

accepted_answers: The value in the middle of an ordered list

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Find the median of 7, 12, 11, 6, 2.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Takes the middle term from the unsorted list instead of ordering first.

cognitive_load.justification: Uses the exact source example and requires ordering before choosing the middle value.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Find the median of 10, 12, 7.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Chooses 12 because it is the largest or 7 because it is first after a partial sort.

cognitive_load.justification: Keeps the median process short with three values while still requiring ordering.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Find the median of 4, 10, 8, 6.

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Chooses one of the two middle values instead of finding their midpoint.

cognitive_load.justification: Introduces the even-number-of-values case where the midpoint of two central values is needed.

source.source_file: (PART A) N1-3 Secondary Ready - Applications of Numeracy (FOUNDATION Not Meeting Expected Standard) - Answers.pptx

question.stem: Christopher has a twin brother aged 12 and two older brothers aged 18 and 14. What is the median age of the four brothers?

misconceptions.diagnostic_signal: Picks 12 or 14 as the median instead of averaging the two middle ages.

cognitive_load.justification: Reteach applies the even-case median method in a short worded context from the source slide.
